metadata.name: Mary Alberti
metadata.protocol: 4-direction expert panel verification (Demographer, Behavioral Economist, Political Scientist, Psychologist)

direction_1_seed_vs_interview.description: Seed data compared against interview transcript for consistency, enrichment, and contradiction.

demographer.detail: Seed states age 28, female, never married, Madison WI 53717, high school education, fast food/counter worker. Interview confirms all of these identically (Q84, Q85, Q87, Q89, Q90, Q08).

Seed states 'second-generation Italian-American raised in Madison, Wisconsin.' Interview enriches: paternal grandparents came from a town south of Naples; maternal family is longstanding Wisconsin (Q01, Q28, Q89).
Seed mentions 'modest, Catholic, Midwestern upbringing.' Interview specifies St. Maria Goretti parish, family attendance since grandparents' generation, nonna was 'very devout' (Q47, Q94).

Seed states parents with no detail. Interview provides: father Tony (63, retired machinist, chronic back injury), mother Linda (61, part-time bookkeeper at dental office), brother Marco (25, HVAC tech in Milwaukee) (Q05, Q100-Q107).
Seed gives DOB-implied age of 28. Interview gives DOB July 14, 1997, and explicitly states 'I'm twenty-eight now' (Q01, Q85). Consistent.

behavioral_economist.detail: Seed describes 'habit of double-checking every receipt for hidden costs.' Interview traces this to dad's back injury period: 'I remember checking every receipt and cutting coupons -- that's actually where I got the habit' (Q02 follow-up). Provides causal origin story.

Seed mentions 'saving for a future diner venture.' Interview quantifies: diner fund at $1,200, emergency fund at ~$600, credit card debt of $800, take-home ~$1,900/month. Provides full financial picture (Q56, Q57, Q61, Q63, Q72, Q82).

Seed states 'plans to enroll in community college hospitality program and obtain ServSafe manager certification.' Interview confirms both goals and adds detail about researching Madison College courses online (Q01 follow-up, Q18, Q91, Q108).

Seed calls Mary 'routine-obsessed, bullet-journal aficionado.' Interview provides extensive detail: color-coded system (blue=work, green=personal, red=money), daily 20-minute ritual, 4+ years, full shelf of filled journals, tracks running miles, spending, garden tasks, meal plans (Q03, Q15, Q17, Q56).

Seed mentions 'occasional craft-brew indulgence' in persona. Interview confirms craft brewery tastings with Jess, 2-3 drinks/week max, tracks spending on it in journal (Q07, Q38). No contradiction, but seed understates regularity -- it is a consistent social ritual, not merely 'occasional.'

political_scientist.detail: Seed contains no political information. Interview provides rich political detail: votes in presidential elections, leans center-left on pocketbook issues, avoids partisan identification, reads NPR occasionally, relies on Jess for political information (Q23-Q30, Q95-Q96).

Seed mentions 'Catholic Midwestern upbringing.' Interview shows religion is maintained through church attendance but does not drive political views -- Mary's civic orientation is pragmatic/economic rather than faith-based (Q47, Q95, Q96).

Seed mentions no civic engagement. Interview reveals: votes in presidential elections, sometimes misses local elections due to work schedule, early votes when possible, participates in community garden, Madison Runners Club member, attends church (Q23, Q08, Q07, Q47).

Seed mentions no racial or social justice engagement. Interview reveals empathy-from-exposure pattern: conversations with coworker DeShawn shifted awareness, reads articles Jess sends, pushes back on ignorant comments at work quietly (Q26-Q30).

psychologist.detail: Seed persona describes Mary as 'routine-obsessed' and 'disciplined.' Interview reveals underlying behavioral patterns: anticipatory rumination before decisions (lying awake running scenarios, 1-2x/month since high school), need for control through systems (Q03, Q31, Q45). The routines serve as self-regulation mechanisms.

Seed mentions 'competitive edge.' Interview contextualizes: competitive with self rather than others (tracking miles, hitting performance targets, Sudoku for mental challenge), motivated by competence demonstration rather than social competition (Q15, Q16, Q65).

Seed states 'calmly resolve high-pressure customer issues.' Interview provides behavioral coping mechanism: 'I just take a breath and fix it. Getting upset doesn't help anything' -- consistent pattern of emotion regulation through action (Q16 follow-up, Q44, Q34).

Seed does not mention seasonal mood patterns. Interview reveals pronounced winter energy dips (late Jan-early Feb), reduced motivation, flattened engagement, bounces back with spring/outdoor activity (Q43, Q50). This is a significant behavioral pattern not captured in seed data.

Seed mentions general self-help reading. Interview reveals this serves identity maintenance: picks up specific techniques (e.g., 'one good thing' gratitude entry) and integrates them into journal system, reflecting a pattern of structured self-improvement (Q50).

Seed arts_persona mentions 'attending local art walks and museum exhibits.' Interview provides no direct evidence of this behavior -- only Wes Anderson films and music are mentioned. This is flagged as an unresolved item in the existing extracted profile's others_misc section. The behavior may exist but was not elicited.

direction_2_interview_vs_extracted_profi.description: Interview transcript compared against extracted_profile for accuracy, omissions, and misrepresentations.

demographer.detail: Profile header demographics (age 28, female, never_married, Madison WI 53717, high school diploma, fast food crew member) all match interview exactly (Q84-Q90, Q08, Q16).

Profile demographer summary correctly places Mary's annual household income at $23,000-$25,000 gross. Interview states take-home ~$1,900/month (Q72) and pre-deduction ~$2,200-$2,300/month (Q72), which yields $26,400-$27,600 gross annually. The profile estimate is slightly low but within reasonable range.
Profile does not record Mary's date of birth (July 14, 1997) from Q85. Should be included for completeness.
Profile does not record Mary's race/ethnicity explicitly in the header. Interview states 'White. Italian-American' (Q89). Should be in demographics.
Profile demographer summary mentions mother's associate's degree but does not note father's education level (high school, Q100). Minor but should be recorded for completeness.

Profile demographer summary states 'annual household income of approximately $23,000-$25,000 gross.' Actual calculation from interview: pre-deduction $2,200-$2,300/month x 12 = $26,400-$27,600 gross (Q72). Should be corrected to ~$26,000-$28,000.

behavioral_economist.detail: Profile accurately captures bullet-journal commitment device, loss aversion around emergency/diner funds, future-oriented time preferences, cost-minimization behaviors. All match interview evidence.
Profile does not capture the tax refund ($300-$400) as a behavioral savings mechanism -- Mary directs it to the diner fund (Q75). This is a notable budgeting strategy.
Profile does not capture the automatic savings transfer from direct deposit (Q73). This is a commitment device consistent with the behavioral economist's analysis.

Profile correctly identifies identity utility tied to competence and self-reliance as barrier to government assistance (Q80). Interview confirms: 'I feel like those programs are for people who are worse off than me.'
Profile work_and_career section lists financial details but does not capture the credit card payoff target (zero by August) from Q63 follow-up. This is a concrete budgeting commitment.

political_scientist.detail: Profile accurately captures low-engagement, economically motivated voting pattern, center-left lean on pocketbook issues, avoidance of partisan identity. Matches Q23-Q25, Q95-Q96.

Profile community_and_civic section does not mention Mary's specific policy interests: minimum wage, healthcare costs, tipped wages and fast food worker protections (Q23 follow-up, Q24, Q25 follow-up). These are her primary political motivators.

Profile does not capture Mary's specific information sources for political decisions: NPR, local news accounts online, Jess as political information intermediary (Q23 follow-up, Q48). This is partially in media_and_information_diet but not connected to civic behavior.
Profile accurately captures empathy-from-exposure pattern on racial issues through coworker interactions (Q26-Q30) and internal processing style.

psychologist.detail: Profile psychologist summary accurately captures high conscientiousness, moderate introversion, anticipatory rumination, seasonal mood dips, and self-regulation through exercise/journaling/cooking. Matches Q03, Q31, Q43, Q44, Q45.

Profile does not capture Mary's specific emotional processing pattern after the breakup: physical activity first (running at night), then tactile activity (cooking pasta while crying), then structured recovery (batch cooking, work focus) (Q44). This is a distinctive three-phase coping sequence.

Profile does not capture Mary's comparison-to-peers pattern: 'I'll start comparing myself to people from high school who went to college and have real careers' (Q45 follow-up). This is a significant source of distress that she actively counters.

Profile correctly identifies mild avoidance patterns around professional help intersecting financial constraints with identity-based resistance. Matches Q32, Q36, Q45 follow-up where Jess suggests sliding-scale therapy and Mary resists.

Profile does not capture Mary's narrative identity link between father's physical deterioration and her own career motivation: 'He's a cautionary tale... I just don't want to end up where he is' (Q35 follow-up). This is a key motivational driver.

Profile does not capture Mary's fear of stagnation as her primary anxiety: 'That I'll still be in the same place ten years from now... I'm afraid of hard work that doesn't lead anywhere' (Q108 follow-up). This is the central existential concern.

direction_3_profile_internal_consistency.description: Extracted profile checked for internal contradictions, logical inconsistencies, and cross-section coherence.

demographer.detail: Profile demographer summary states annual income of '$23,000-$25,000 gross' but the work_and_career section states take-home of ~$1,900/month and pre-deduction of $2,200-$2,300/month (from Q72), which would yield $26,400-$27,600 gross. The summary understates income by ~$2,000-$3,000.

All demographic fields in the header are internally consistent with the body of the profile. Age, location, education, occupation, and marital status are referenced consistently across all 15 preference categories and 4 summaries.

behavioral_economist.detail: Financial figures across sections are internally consistent: rent $900, groceries $250-$300, car costs $200, insurance $120, utilities $70-$80, phone $50 = ~$1,590-$1,650 in fixed costs against $1,900 take-home, leaving $250-$310 for variable expenses and savings. Matches the 'hundred to two hundred for savings' stated in Q57.

Behavioral patterns are internally coherent: loss aversion (emergency fund anxiety), present-bias mitigation (journal system), cost-minimization (receipt-checking, coupon use, cheap meals), identity-based resistance to assistance -- all form a unified behavioral profile.

Profile describes craft beer as 'small identity-affirming expenditures she carefully budgets' in the behavioral economist summary, but the hobbies section says 'approximately weekly when budget allows.' These are not contradictory but the framing differs -- one emphasizes the identity function, the other the frequency constraint. Both are accurate.

political_scientist.detail: Profile's political characterization ('low-engagement, economically motivated voter') is consistent with the civic participation details (votes in presidential elections, misses some local ones, no rallies/protests, processes internally).
The community_and_civic section and political_scientist summary are mutually reinforcing: both describe quiet participation, internal processing, and practical rather than ideological motivation.

psychologist.detail: Psychological profile is internally coherent: high conscientiousness drives the journaling, planning, and organizational systems; moderate introversion explains the small social circle and internal processing; the self-regulation mechanisms (running, cooking, journaling) are consistently described across multiple preference categories.

The health_and_wellness section's mention of lying awake before decisions (1-2x/month) is consistent with the psychologist summary's 'anticipatory rumination' and the emotional well-being data from Q45. Cross-references are accurate.

Profile psychologist summary says 'moderate-low openness' and notes she 'gravitates toward routine, familiarity, and practical knowledge over novelty-seeking.' However, the same summary acknowledges she 'appreciates indie music and Wes Anderson films,' and interview reveals she tries new craft breweries, researches new recipes, reads self-help for new techniques. Openness may be better characterized as 'moderate' -- she seeks novelty within structured, familiar frameworks.

direction_4_completeness.description: Profile checked against full 109-question transcript for missing information that should be captured.

demographer.detail: Date of birth (July 14, 1997) from Q85 not included in profile header.
Race/ethnicity ('White, Italian-American') from Q89 not included in profile header.
Birth city and hospital (Madison, Wisconsin, St. Mary's Hospital) from Q87 not in profile.
Religion (Catholic, attends St. Maria Goretti) from Q94 not in profile header -- only in preference sections and summaries.

behavioral_economist.detail: Tax refund as savings mechanism ($300-$400 directed to diner fund) from Q75 not captured in financial profile.
Automatic savings transfer from direct deposit (Q73) not captured as a commitment device.
Credit card payoff target (zero by August, $50/month payments) from Q63 not captured as a concrete goal.
Garden season costs (~$50 for season for soil/seeds/plant food, plus $40 plot rental) from Q59 and Q08 follow-up not captured in budget detail.
Holiday spending pattern (December deficit, recovery by February) from Q59 not captured as a seasonal financial cycle.

political_scientist.detail: Specific policy priorities (minimum wage, healthcare costs, tipped wages/fast food worker protections) from Q23 follow-up, Q24, Q25 follow-up not listed in community_and_civic preferences.
Mary's father's more conservative/self-reliant political orientation vs. Mary's and Jess's more progressive lean (Q24 follow-up) not captured as intergenerational political dynamic.
Mary's specific racial awareness evolution (DeShawn's story about walking home from closing shifts, Q26 follow-up) is rich civic/social detail not fully captured.

psychologist.detail: Three-phase emotional coping sequence (physical activity -> tactile/creative activity -> structured recovery) from Q44 not documented as a behavioral pattern.

Social comparison pattern and active counter-strategy ('I'll start comparing myself to people from high school... then I have to snap out of it and remind myself I've got a plan') from Q45 follow-up not documented.
Father-as-cautionary-tale motivation ('He's a cautionary tale... I just don't want to end up where he is') from Q35 follow-up not documented as a key motivational driver.
Central existential fear of stagnation ('That I'll still be in the same place ten years from now... I'm afraid of hard work that doesn't lead anywhere') from Q108 follow-up not documented.

Stoic work culture norm ('In food service, everybody's dealing with something -- sore feet, tired, hungry. You just push through') from Q34 follow-up not documented as a behavioral norm she internalizes.

expert_summaries.demographer: Mary Alberti is a 28-year-old (DOB July 14, 1997) never-married white female of Italian-American descent, born and raised in Madison, Wisconsin. She holds a high school diploma from East High (2015) and works as a fast food crew member performing de facto shift-lead duties, placing her in the service sector working class with an annual gross household income of approximately $26,000-$28,000. She lives alone in a rented one-bedroom apartment on Madison's west side (ZIP 53717), is not Hispanic/Latinx, and identifies as Catholic, attending St. Maria Goretti parish. She is second-generation Italian-American with paternal grandparents who immigrated from southern Italy (a town south of Naples); her maternal family has deep Wisconsin roots. Her father Tony (63) is a retired machinist with chronic back injury, her mother Linda (61) is a part-time bookkeeper with an associate's degree in accounting, and her brother Marco (25) works in HVAC in Milwaukee. Both parents hold high-school-level education, with Linda holding an additional associate's degree. The family's trajectory -- from immigrant manual labor through skilled manufacturing to service-sector employment, intersected by a disabling occupational injury -- reflects patterns common to blue-collar Midwestern families navigating deindustrialization. Mary is at a pivotal life-course juncture: pre-partnered, still in the parental orbit, and in the early phases of credentialing (planning Madison College hospitality program) that could shift her occupational trajectory from hourly service work to small business ownership.
expert_summaries.behavioral_economist: Mary displays strong present-bias mitigation through her bullet-journal system, which functions as a multi-domain commitment device for budgeting, scheduling, goal-tracking, and emotional regulation. She maintains two distinct savings pools -- an emergency fund (~$600) and a dedicated diner fund (~$1,200) -- and employs automatic savings transfers from direct deposit and channels annual tax refunds ($300-$400) directly to the diner fund (Q73, Q75). She exhibits pronounced loss aversion around fund depletion events, showing visible distress at emergency-fund drawdowns for brake repairs and sick-day income loss (Q60, Q63, Q64). Her time preferences are consistently future-oriented: she defers gratification by skipping social outings, substituting cheap meals (rice/beans, pasta e fagioli), and delaying purchases (running shoes) to maintain savings targets. She has set a concrete credit card payoff target of zero by August at $50/month (Q63). Peer influence operates through a single strong-tie channel (Jess) for both consumption (craft beer tastings) and information (political news, health advice). Her identity utility is heavily tied to competence and self-reliance, creating measurable resistance to government assistance programs despite likely eligibility -- she frames this as 'those programs are for people who are worse off than me' (Q80). She responds to financial constraint through cost-minimization (receipt-checking, coupon use, home cooking from garden produce, negotiating payment plans) rather than income-maximization (she rejected a second job due to burnout risk). Risk aversion is moderate: she declined the Milwaukee transfer despite higher pay, weighting social capital and local knowledge over income gains (Q02). Seasonal financial cycles show a December deficit from holiday spending with recovery by February (Q59). Small identity-affirming expenditures (craft beer, running shoes) are carefully budgeted and tracked in the journal.
expert_summaries.political_scientist: Mary is a low-engagement, economically motivated voter who participates in presidential elections and occasionally in local ones, using early voting when possible to work around her unpredictable shift schedule (Q23). She avoids partisan identification but leans center-left on pocketbook issues -- her primary policy motivators are minimum wage increases, healthcare affordability, and tipped wages/fast food worker protections (Q23 follow-up, Q24, Q25 follow-up). She expresses frustration with political polarization and gravitates toward candidates who 'help regular people who are working hard and still struggling to get ahead' (Q23 follow-up). Her political information diet is narrow: she reads NPR occasionally, follows local Madison news accounts online, and relies heavily on Jess as a political information intermediary (Q23 follow-up, Q48). Civic participation beyond voting is passive: she attends Catholic mass at St. Maria Goretti, participates in a community garden and running club, but does not attend protests, rallies, or political events, processing political and social issues internally and discussing them only with close friends (Q29). Her response to racial justice movements reflects an empathy-from-exposure pattern: direct workplace conversations with her coworker DeShawn about differential policing experiences shifted her awareness, and she will quietly push back on ignorant comments at work, but she has not translated this into public action (Q26-Q30). An intergenerational political dynamic is present: her father Tony holds more conservative, self-reliant views ('tough it out'), her mother Linda is more sympathetic to systemic difficulties, and Mary positions herself in the middle, pragmatic rather than ideological (Q24 follow-up). Institutional trust is low regarding healthcare and workplace protections, moderate regarding electoral participation, and strong regarding vaccination science. Community attachment to Madison is exceptionally strong, serving as both an identity anchor and a constraint on geographic mobility for economic opportunity.
expert_summaries.psychologist: Mary exhibits high conscientiousness expressed through meticulous planning, routine adherence, goal persistence, receipt-tracking, organizational systems, and a four-year bullet-journal practice that functions as her primary self-regulation tool (Q03, Q17). She displays moderate introversion: she prefers one-on-one interactions to group settings, processes emotions internally, maintains a small social circle of one close friend plus family, and dislikes public expression of personal views (Q07, Q29, Q49). Her stress response follows a distinctive three-phase behavioral coping sequence: first physical activity (running, even at night after the breakup), then tactile/creative engagement (cooking, gardening), then structured recovery through journaling and planning (Q44, Q37 follow-up, Q50). Anticipatory rumination manifests before significant decisions as racing thoughts and insomnia (1-2x/month, present since high school), and she experiences pronounced seasonal energy dips in late January-early February with reduced motivation and flattened engagement, recovering reliably with spring and renewed outdoor activity (Q31, Q43, Q45). She actively counters a social comparison pattern -- comparing herself to college-educated high school peers -- by redirecting attention to her own plan and progress (Q45 follow-up). Her self-concept is organized around competence, self-reliance, and delayed gratification, with a narrative identity that links her grandmother's immigrant work ethic ('A poco a poco') to her own aspirations (Q109 follow-up). A key motivational driver is her father's physical deterioration from decades of manual labor, which she explicitly frames as a cautionary parallel to her own situation and a reason to pursue management rather than remaining on the line (Q35 follow-up). Her central existential fear is stagnation: 'That I'll still be in the same place ten years from now... I'm afraid of hard work that doesn't lead anywhere' (Q108 follow-up). She internalizes a stoic workplace norm ('In food service, everybody's dealing with something... You just push through') that reinforces her resistance to seeking professional help, intersecting with financial constraints and identity-based reluctance to be seen as someone who needs external support (Q34 follow-up, Q45 follow-up). Agreeableness is moderate: empathetic and conflict-avoidant in social settings but firm about time boundaries and priorities. Openness is moderate -- she seeks novelty within structured, familiar frameworks (trying new craft breweries and recipes, reading self-help for applicable techniques, appreciating indie music and Wes Anderson films), but gravitates toward routine and practical knowledge over abstract exploration.

inconsistencies_found.description: Demographer summary states annual gross income as $23,000-$25,000 but interview data yields $26,400-$27,600 gross based on $2,200-$2,300/month pre-deduction (Q72).
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Corrected to approximately $26,000-$28,000 gross in demographer summary and work_and_career details.

inconsistencies_found.description: Psychologist summary rates openness as 'moderate-low' but interview evidence shows novelty-seeking within structured frameworks (new breweries, new recipes, self-help techniques). More accurately 'moderate.'
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Corrected to 'moderate' in psychologist summary with qualification about structured novelty-seeking.

inconsistencies_found.description: Seed describes craft beer as 'occasional' indulgence, but interview shows it is a regular weekly social ritual with Jess, carefully budgeted.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Profile already accurately reflects the weekly pattern. No correction needed in profile; noted for seed-to-interview reconciliation.

Missing political detail: specific policy priorities, intergenerational political dynamic, DeShawn anecdote.
gaps_addressed.action: Added to community_and_civic details and political_scientist summary.

gaps_addressed.description: Missing psychological patterns: three-phase coping sequence, social comparison pattern, father-as-cautionary-tale, stagnation fear, stoic work norm.
gaps_addressed.action: Added to psychologist summary and others_misc behavioral_patterns subcategory.

gaps_addressed.description: Missing emotional/motivational detail from interview that should inform the profile.
gaps_addressed.action: Incorporated into psychologist summary and health_and_wellness details.

gaps_addressed.description: Date of birth from Q85 not in profile.
gaps_addressed.action: Added date_of_birth field to demographics header.

verification.overall_verdict: The existing extracted_profile is substantially accurate and well-structured. No major contradictions were found between the seed data, interview transcript, and extracted profile. The primary issues are: (1) a moderate income estimate error in the demographer summary ($23K-$25K should be $26K-$28K), (2) several demographic fields missing from the header (DOB, race/ethnicity, religion, birth city, Hispanic/Latinx), (3) a number of meaningful behavioral and psychological patterns from the interview not captured in the profile (three-phase coping, social comparison, father-as-cautionary-tale, stagnation fear, stoic work norm), (4) missing financial detail (tax refund routing, auto-save, credit card target, seasonal cycles), and (5) the psychologist's openness rating adjusted from moderate-low to moderate. All 109 questions were reviewed. The corrected profile addresses all identified gaps and inconsistencies.

corrected_extracted_profile.name: Mary Alberti
corrected_extracted_profile.education: High school diploma (East High, 2015); planning to enroll at Madison College for hospitality management
corrected_extracted_profile.occupation: Fast food crew member (de facto shift lead), 3 years at current location, ~10 years total in food service

health_and_wellness.summary: Active runner who manages health through routine, home remedies, and budget-conscious choices; avoids medical visits due to cost and self-reliance identity. Uses a three-phase coping sequence (physical activity, tactile/creative engagement, structured planning) to process stress.
details: Runs 3-5 miles multiple times per week, including Saturday mornings with the Madison Runners Club around Lake Mendota
Experiences tension headaches in temples/back of neck during stressful periods; manages with Advil and peppermint oil on temples
Knee soreness from running for about 3-4 months, especially past 4 miles; manages with 15-minute post-run stretching and icing
Dairy sensitivity causing bloating; self-diagnosed by tracking food intake, reduced dairy consumption (still eats cheese in moderation -- 'this is Wisconsin')
Switched from coffee to green tea (Bigelow, from Pick 'n Save) about a month ago to reduce jitteriness during early shifts
Uses Italian grandmother's home remedies: garlic-honey for sore throat, herbal compresses for muscle aches, garlic broth for colds
Peppermint oil on temples for headaches (discovered online, finds effective)
Hasn't had a dental visit in over a year; no regular checkup in about two years due to cost and high-deductible insurance ($2,000 deductible)
Gets flu shot annually at Walgreens; up to date on recommended vaccinations; trusts CDC website and healthcare providers
Anticipatory rumination before big decisions: lying awake running through scenarios, racing heart, 1-2 times per month, present since high school (Q31, Q45)
Seasonal energy dips in late January-early February: reduced motivation, flattened engagement, minimal journal entries; reliably recovers with spring and outdoor activity (Q43)
Three-phase emotional coping sequence: (1) physical activity (running), (2) tactile/creative engagement (cooking, gardening), (3) structured recovery (journaling, planning) (Q44, Q37 follow-up, Q50)
Finds gardening and running therapeutic for stress; explicitly says 'running is my therapy' (Q45 follow-up)
Foot soreness after long (8+ hour) shifts from standing all day (Q34)
Had a significant cold in January that cost 3 lost shifts (~$250 lost wages) and no paid sick days (Q33)

food_and_dining.summary: Accomplished home cook specializing in Italian-Midwestern fusion comfort food; meticulous meal prepper who grows herbs and vegetables in a community garden. Food is both a cultural connection to her Italian-American heritage and a coping mechanism during stress.
details: Cooks most meals from scratch; Sunday meal prep is a weekly ritual (chopping vegetables, making sauces, portioning for the week)
Specialties: nonna's red sauce, braciole, cheddar-crusted bratwurst, cheese-curd poutine, spiced apple crumble, pasta e fagioli ($4 per pot, feeds her for 3 days)
Taught herself grandmother's recipes after nonna's passing to preserve the family tradition (Q01 follow-up)
Grows tomatoes, basil, green beans, and herbs in a rented raised-bed community garden plot ($40/season, 3 years running)
Shops at Hy-Vee and Pick 'n Save; visits Dane County Farmers Market on Capitol Square in season
Spends $250-$300/month on groceries; cuts to rice-and-beans meals, ramen-and-eggs, or pasta with garlic and oil when budget is tight
Employee meal discount (50% off) during shifts at fast food restaurant
Enjoys hosting low-key dinner gatherings with friend Jess (Jess brings wine, Mary cooks)
Italian-American Sunday dinner tradition from childhood; still cooks family recipes to maintain cultural connection
Views cooking cheap, satisfying meals as a small victory: 'Making a really good meal out of cheap ingredients feels like winning' (Q83 follow-up)
Breakfast varies by shift: quick (toast, banana, yogurt, oatmeal) for early shifts; bigger breakfast when on closing shift

travel.summary: Prefers affordable, planned weekend getaways within Wisconsin; travel aspirations constrained by tight budget and prioritization of diner savings. Has never left the Midwest for extended periods.
details: Prefers meticulously planned weekend getaways balancing relaxation and culinary experiences
Door County lakeside trips as aspirational local destination
Napa Valley culinary adventure as a bigger dream trip
Kyoto as long-term overseas dream destination
Travel severely constrained by tight budget; balances costs against diner savings goal
Has never left the Midwest/Madison area for extended periods
Chose to stay in Madison over transferring to Milwaukee for higher pay, prioritizing roots over mobility (Q02)
Strong community attachment to Madison as identity anchor: 'Everything I care about is here' (Q10 follow-up)
travel.evidence: Q01, Q02, Q10, Q108 (travel persona from seed data; limited direct interview evidence -- aspirational preferences from seed not contradicted but constrained by financial reality in interview)

social_and_relationships.summary: Small, tight social circle centered on one best friend, parents, and a running group; values deep one-on-one connections over large social gatherings. Processes relationship stress through physical activity and cooking rather than talking.
details: Best friend Jess (dental hygienist, high school friend): weekly hangouts, craft brewery tastings, home dinners, primary emotional support, political information source, occasionally crashes on Mary's couch

Parents Tony (63, retired machinist, chronic back pain) and Linda (61, part-time bookkeeper at dental office): daily phone calls with mom (usually mom's lunch break or after Mary's shift), Sunday Packers games with dad, frequent visits 2-3x/week for errands/company
Brother Marco (25, HVAC tech, Milwaukee): weekly texts, holiday visits, amicable but not close -- 'He's more spontaneous, more social'; they argue about Brewers' pitching staff (Q05 follow-up)
Madison Runners Club: Saturday morning regulars Dave and Priya; social but not deep friendships -- 2 years of membership
Work friendships are shift-only; does not socialize with coworkers outside of work, though has meaningful conversations with coworker DeShawn about policing experiences (Q26)
Church congregation at St. Maria Goretti provides light social contact: the Morettis, the Petersons, old Mrs. Gustafson (Q47 follow-up)
Maternal grandparents alive in Wausau; seen at Thanksgiving and Christmas; aunts/uncles/cousins not close (Q06)
Single after 6-month relationship ended ~8 months ago; ex-boyfriend didn't understand her schedule rigidity; not actively dating (Q05)
Processes relationship stress through physical activity (night run after breakup), cooking (cried while making pasta), and batch-cooking/work focus (Q44)
Very private: never posts personal problems online, only confides in Jess and mom (Q49)

home_and_lifestyle.summary: Lives alone in a modest one-bedroom apartment on Madison's west side; highly organized, routine-driven household with bullet-journal planning at its center. Seasonal cost variations (winter heating, holiday spending) create financial pressure.
details: One-bedroom apartment, second floor of a two-story building, west side Madison (53717), near Midvale/beltline area
Rent: $900/month (up from $750 four years ago); decent landlord who fixes things
Drives a 2015 Honda Civic ('beat-up, gets the job done'); needed for variable shift times not on bus routes

Bullet journal system: color-coded daily/weekly/monthly spreads (blue=work, green=personal, red=money); tracks running miles, spending, garden tasks, meal plans; daily 20-minute ritual; 4+ years; full shelf of filled journals (Q17 follow-up)
Added gratitude practice: writes one thing that went well each day (Q50, from a self-help book)

Two routine versions: opening-shift routine (up 5:30, out 6:15, work 7-2/3) and closing-shift routine (sleep till 9, errands/run in morning, work 3-11); back-to-back close/open shifts yield ~5 hours sleep (Q15, Q78 follow-up)

summaries.demographer: Mary Alberti is a 28-year-old (DOB July 14, 1997) never-married white female of Italian-American descent, born and raised in Madison, Wisconsin. She holds a high school diploma from East High (2015) and works as a fast food crew member performing de facto shift-lead duties, placing her in the service sector working class with an annual gross household income of approximately $26,000-$28,000. She lives alone in a rented one-bedroom apartment on Madison's west side (ZIP 53717), is not Hispanic/Latinx, and identifies as Catholic, attending St. Maria Goretti parish. She is second-generation Italian-American with paternal grandparents who immigrated from southern Italy (a town south of Naples); her maternal family has deep Wisconsin roots. Her father Tony (63) is a retired machinist with chronic back injury, her mother Linda (61) is a part-time bookkeeper with an associate's degree in accounting, and her brother Marco (25) works in HVAC in Milwaukee. Both parents hold high-school-level education, with Linda holding an additional associate's degree. The family's trajectory -- from immigrant manual labor through skilled manufacturing to service-sector employment, intersected by a disabling occupational injury -- reflects patterns common to blue-collar Midwestern families navigating deindustrialization. Mary is at a pivotal life-course juncture: pre-partnered, still in the parental orbit, and in the early phases of credentialing (planning Madison College hospitality program) that could shift her occupational trajectory from hourly service work to small business ownership.
summaries.behavioral_economist: Mary displays strong present-bias mitigation through her bullet-journal system, which functions as a multi-domain commitment device for budgeting, scheduling, goal-tracking, and emotional regulation. She maintains two distinct savings pools -- an emergency fund (~$600) and a dedicated diner fund (~$1,200) -- and employs automatic savings transfers from direct deposit and channels annual tax refunds ($300-$400) directly to the diner fund (Q73, Q75). She exhibits pronounced loss aversion around fund depletion events, showing visible distress at emergency-fund drawdowns for brake repairs and sick-day income loss (Q60, Q63, Q64). Her time preferences are consistently future-oriented: she defers gratification by skipping social outings, substituting cheap meals (rice/beans, pasta e fagioli), and delaying purchases (running shoes) to maintain savings targets. She has set a concrete credit card payoff target of zero by August at $50/month (Q63). Peer influence operates through a single strong-tie channel (Jess) for both consumption (craft beer tastings) and information (political news, health advice). Her identity utility is heavily tied to competence and self-reliance, creating measurable resistance to government assistance programs despite likely eligibility -- she frames this as 'those programs are for people who are worse off than me' (Q80). She responds to financial constraint through cost-minimization (receipt-checking, coupon use, home cooking from garden produce, negotiating payment plans) rather than income-maximization (she rejected a second job due to burnout risk). Risk aversion is moderate: she declined the Milwaukee transfer despite higher pay, weighting social capital and local knowledge over income gains (Q02). Seasonal financial cycles show a December deficit from holiday spending with recovery by February (Q59). Small identity-affirming expenditures (craft beer, running shoes) are carefully budgeted and tracked in the journal.
summaries.political_scientist: Mary is a low-engagement, economically motivated voter who participates in presidential elections and occasionally in local ones, using early voting when possible to work around her unpredictable shift schedule (Q23). She avoids partisan identification but leans center-left on pocketbook issues -- her primary policy motivators are minimum wage increases, healthcare affordability, and tipped wages/fast food worker protections (Q23 follow-up, Q24, Q25 follow-up). She expresses frustration with political polarization and gravitates toward candidates who 'help regular people who are working hard and still struggling to get ahead' (Q23 follow-up). Her political information diet is narrow: she reads NPR occasionally, follows local Madison news accounts online, and relies heavily on Jess as a political information intermediary (Q23 follow-up, Q48). Civic participation beyond voting is passive: she attends Catholic mass at St. Maria Goretti, participates in a community garden and running club, but does not attend protests, rallies, or political events, processing political and social issues internally and discussing them only with close friends (Q29). Her response to racial justice movements reflects an empathy-from-exposure pattern: direct workplace conversations with her coworker DeShawn about differential policing experiences shifted her awareness, and she will quietly push back on ignorant comments at work, but she has not translated this into public action (Q26-Q30). An intergenerational political dynamic is present: her father Tony holds more conservative, self-reliant views ('tough it out'), her mother Linda is more sympathetic to systemic difficulties, and Mary positions herself in the middle, pragmatic rather than ideological (Q24 follow-up). Institutional trust is low regarding healthcare and workplace protections, moderate regarding electoral participation, and strong regarding vaccination science. Community attachment to Madison is exceptionally strong, serving as both an identity anchor and a constraint on geographic mobility for economic opportunity.
summaries.psychologist: Mary exhibits high conscientiousness expressed through meticulous planning, routine adherence, goal persistence, receipt-tracking, organizational systems, and a four-year bullet-journal practice that functions as her primary self-regulation tool (Q03, Q17). She displays moderate introversion: she prefers one-on-one interactions to group settings, processes emotions internally, maintains a small social circle of one close friend plus family, and dislikes public expression of personal views (Q07, Q29, Q49). Her stress response follows a distinctive three-phase behavioral coping sequence: first physical activity (running, even at night after the breakup), then tactile/creative engagement (cooking, gardening), then structured recovery through journaling and planning (Q44, Q37 follow-up, Q50). Anticipatory rumination manifests before significant decisions as racing thoughts and insomnia (1-2x/month, present since high school), and she experiences pronounced seasonal energy dips in late January-early February with reduced motivation and flattened engagement, recovering reliably with spring and renewed outdoor activity (Q31, Q43, Q45). She actively counters a social comparison pattern -- comparing herself to college-educated high school peers -- by redirecting attention to her own plan and progress (Q45 follow-up). Her self-concept is organized around competence, self-reliance, and delayed gratification, with a narrative identity that links her grandmother's immigrant work ethic ('A poco a poco') to her own aspirations (Q109 follow-up). A key motivational driver is her father's physical deterioration from decades of manual labor, which she explicitly frames as a cautionary parallel to her own situation and a reason to pursue management rather than remaining on the line (Q35 follow-up). Her central existential fear is stagnation: 'That I'll still be in the same place ten years from now... I'm afraid of hard work that doesn't lead anywhere' (Q108 follow-up). She internalizes a stoic workplace norm ('In food service, everybody's dealing with something... You just push through') that reinforces her resistance to seeking professional help, intersecting with financial constraints and identity-based reluctance to be seen as someone who needs external support (Q34 follow-up, Q45 follow-up). Agreeableness is moderate: empathetic and conflict-avoidant in social settings but firm about time boundaries and priorities. Openness is moderate -- she seeks novelty within structured, familiar frameworks (trying new craft breweries and recipes, reading self-help for applicable techniques, appreciating indie music and Wes Anderson films), but gravitates toward routine and practical knowledge over abstract exploration.
